content_summary.core_message: Base training is the essential foundation for all future running performance. It builds durability, aerobic efficiency, and consistency while preventing injury.
key_takeaways: Base training transitions you from recovery or inactivity into structured running.
Volume must increase gradually and sustainably.
Heart rate should stay low to develop aerobic capacity.
Walks and hikes count as base training for slower runners.
Base phase should last 4–12 weeks depending on experience.
training_principles: Keep heart rate in the aerobic zone.
Increase volume only when recovery is good.
Use run–walk strategies to safely extend duration.
Track mileage over 10–14 day cycles, not just weekly.
common_mistakes: Running too fast during base training.
Increasing volume before the body adapts.
Not including brisk walking as part of aerobic development.
Trying to add intensity too early.
myths_busted: Base training is not just slow jogging — it includes structured aerobic development.
Walking does not 'ruin' training; it enhances aerobic capacity for many runners.
You don’t need to follow a strict 7‑day cycle; longer cycles work better for some athletes.

adaptations_targeted: Lower resting and submax heart rate
injury_risks: Overuse injuries from increasing volume too quickly
Soft‑tissue fatigue when aerobic base is weak
recovery_requirements: Hydration and fueling for longer aerobic sessions

training_methods.name: Run–Walk Method
training_methods.description: Alternating running with walking to safely increase total duration.

training_methods.name: Easy Aerobic Running
training_methods.description: Steady, conversational‑pace running to build aerobic capacity.
common_errors: Poor pacing early in the run

running_form_cues.purpose: Reduce wasted energy and improve breathing

running_form_cues.cue: Shorten the stride to keep heart rate low

workout_examples.name: Run–Walk Aerobic Builder
workout_examples.goal: Safely increase total time on feet

advanced: Daily aerobic running with strict HR control

motivational_coach_lines: Your base is your superpower — build it patiently.
Consistency beats intensity in the base phase.
Every easy mile is an investment in your future speed.

encouragement: Nice work — keep that heart rate steady.
You're building durability with every easy mile.
corrections: Ease off the pace to stay aerobic.
Shorten your stride to control heart rate.
explanations: Base training builds the aerobic engine you’ll rely on later.
Walking is part of training — not a setback.

deep_explanations: Base training strengthens your aerobic system, connective tissues, and running economy. It’s the safest way to prepare for harder training later.
training_theory: The aerobic zone is where your body becomes more efficient at using oxygen and fat for fuel. Staying here during base training maximizes long‑term performance.
injury_prevention: Gradual volume increases allow your tendons and ligaments to adapt. Rushing this process is the #1 cause of overuse injuries.

fitness_level: How many days per week are you currently running?
Do you track your heart rate during runs?
goals: Are you training for a specific race?
What distance do you want to improve?
feedback: How did your last long run feel?
Are you recovering well between sessions?

structured_timestamps.coach_commentary: This is your transition phase — build consistency first.
Increase mileage only when recovery is strong.
Stay in Zone 2 — this is where the magic happens.
Walks count — they build aerobic strength without stress.
Commit to 4–12 weeks depending on your goals.

examples: You're doing the right work — stay patient.
This pace is perfect for building your foundation.

persona.name: Coach Pulse
persona.coaching_philosophy: Build the engine first. Speed comes later.
